_schema.description: Item-level ground truth v0 — the canonical layer the checklist engine and RAG buckets serve to users. Each item aggregates the provenance claims in seed-claims.json into a consensus verdict, a contested flag, context-dependent overrides, and price/weight facts. `support` counts claims by verdict; `claimIds` links back to evidence. This is what a new user sees on day one, before their own cohort's feedback starts adjusting `communityStats` (empty now — filled post-launch).
_schema.confidence: high (multi-source agreement or verified) | medium (consistent but unverified) | low (single/weak source)
_schema.contested: true when reputable sources give opposing verdicts; UI should show both sides

meta.provenance: deep-research workflow wf_a4cb7f0c-7f3 (17 sources: 6 university ISS pages, 8 blogs, 2 forums, 1 price-reference), synthesized manually

luggageBaseline.note: Most India→US economy tickets: 2×23 kg checked + 1 cabin. Confirm per airline/fare.

items.detail: Passport, I-20/DS-2019 originals, SEVIS receipt, admission letter, financial proofs, transcripts, immunization records — plus 2-3 photocopies and scanned digital backups. Carry originals on your person; never in checked baggage.

Carry a 1-2 month supply of ongoing prescriptions with a doctor's signed letter listing generic drug names and reason — both for customs legality and because US drug prices are far higher. Note some states reject out-of-state/foreign prescriptions, so the written description matters.

price.note: US drug prices multiples of India; see American Pharmaceutical Review comparison

items.detail: Bring a small starter kit (paracetamol, ibuprofen, antacids, anti-allergics, Dettol, bandages) — familiar brands, and you'll want them before you set up US healthcare. One university advises buying OTC in the US to save space, so keep the kit small rather than a bulk stock.
Get 1-2 spare pairs (and/or a year of contacts) made in India before leaving. Eye exams + glasses in the US run ~$225/pair even with insurance; a fraction of that in India.

price.note: US per pair with insurance; far cheaper in India

items.detail: The single most contested item. Emerging consensus: BRING thermal base layers, wool socks, gloves, a beanie, and a light/transit jacket; BUY the heavy down coat in the US, where it's better suited to sustained sub-zero cold and cheap on Black Friday (down jacket ~$100, boots $60-100). Bring a full heavy coat only if you already own a good one (avoid the US cost) or are headed somewhere remote. Wear your heaviest layer on the flight to save luggage weight.

climate.cold: Full winter gear is essential; the bring-vs-buy split is about where you buy the heavy coat, not whether you need one.
climate.warm: A heavy coat is largely unnecessary; skip it.

intake.fall: You arrive in warmth and have weeks to buy a coat before winter — favors buy-in-US.
intake.spring: You land straight into winter — bring at least a warm jacket for day one.

price.note: US down jacket ~$100; branded winter wear ~2x India but heavy US sales narrow it

items.weightNote: a heavy coat is bulky and weight-costly; wear it on the plane if bringing

items.detail: Contested. Pro-bring (most blogs): a 3-5L cooker is central to Indian cooking and pricier in the US. Pro-buy (forums + community): it's heavy, gets red-flagged and hand-inspected in checked bags, and the effective cost of carrying it roughly equals buying one there — an Instant Pot is $60-80 on US sales, and other Indian students will lend one early on. Rule of thumb: buy in the US if you'll be near an Indian community; bring a small one only if headed somewhere remote.

diet.veg-cooking-heavy: Stronger case to bring/own one early since you'll cook daily.
diet.eats-out: Lower priority; buy later if needed.

housing.dorm: Often no kitchen access — skip initially.
housing.apartment: Needed; decide bring-small vs buy Instant Pot.

items.weightNote: ~2-3 kg + bulky; separate/empty parts and use a TSA lock if bringing

items.detail: Lightweight, hard-to-find-cheaply items are worth bringing — a flat iron tava/griddle, a masala box, a tea strainer, a good knife. Bulky/heavy basics (pans, plates, glasses) are cheap in the US (Walmart/dollar stores) and not worth the weight; buy or ship on arrival. Skip anything electric from India (voltage).

housing.apartment: Buy the bulk of it locally.

items.detail: Bring dry, sealed spices and masalas (turmeric, garam masala, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, hing) — a strong majority view. They're cheaper in India and marked up ~4x at US Indian stores, especially in small towns. Declare them at customs; sealed, dry, commercially-packaged spices are generally admissible. Two sources counsel caution (IDP flags 'plant products'), so keep quantities reasonable and everything sealed.

price.note: Indian groceries ~4x India price in US stores

items.weightNote: light and high-value; declare at CBP

items.detail: Genuinely split, but the practical verdict leans SKIP raw rice/lentils: (1) US customs restricts raw rice and unpackaged lentils (Khapra beetle rule for India), (2) they're heavy and burn your weight budget, (3) they're readily available at US Indian stores even if ~7x India prices. Some blogs still say carry a 3-4 kg bridge supply. If you carry any, keep it small, sealed, commercially packaged, and declared.

price.note: US rice ~$4.58/kg vs ~$0.64/kg India (~7x); still cheap in absolute US terms

items.weightNote: heavy; worst weight-to-value ratio on the list

items.detail: Maggi, ready-to-eat poha/khichdi/upma, MTR-type packets — bring a couple weeks' worth. Sold only at US Indian stores and beloved in the first homesick months. Non-perishable and light.

Favorite non-perishable snacks and homemade dry items (theplas, chutney powders, laddus) that are hard to find or expensive in the US. Universities themselves endorse this. Must be dry, sealed, and declared.

Do NOT pack fresh produce, meat, dairy, seeds, plants, or homemade wet/perishable food. US customs (CBP/USDA) confiscates them and undeclared items risk civil penalties. This is a hard rule, not a preference.

Buy or ship on arrival — strong, unanimous university guidance. US dorm beds are Twin XL (39"×80"), a size your Indian sheets won't fit, and linens are bulky. Order sheets/pillow/blanket online for delivery before move-in. Cheap: sheets $15-25, pillow ~$10, towels ~$15, blanket $20-75.

housing.apartment: You choose the bed; buy bedding locally to match.

items.detail: Do NOT bring Indian 220-240V appliances (rice cookers, kettles, hair dryers, irons, lamps). US is 110-120V/60Hz — they need a bulky transformer and often just fail. They're cheap in the US. Bring only your laptop/phone chargers plus a couple of Type A/B plug adapters.
items.weightNote: transformers are heavy — not worth it

items.detail: Bring your existing working laptop and phone. Buy NEW electronics, headphones, and accessories in the US — significantly cheaper (e.g. iPhone 11 Pro $999 US vs ₹99,900 India) and Black Friday sales in late November are the moment to buy.

price.note: iPhone example; electronics broadly cheaper in US

items.detail: Bring 1-2 universal/Type A-B plug adapters and a power strip so you can charge everything from day one. Cheap and essential — Indian plugs don't fit US sockets.

Bring ~2 weeks of everyday clothes, then shop in the US. Branded apparel and sports shoes are ~2x cheaper in the US with student discounts and Black Friday sales (Nike shoes ~$40 vs ~$90 India). Don't stock up on branded items in India.

price.note: branded clothing/shoes ~2x cheaper in US on sale

items.detail: Bring 1 formal outfit (suit/blazer or a saree/sherwani for cultural events) — tailoring is far cheaper in India, and ethnic wear has limited, costly options in the US. You'll want it for presentations, conferences, and Diwali/cultural nights.

Bring a ~1-month starter kit only; buy the rest in the US where cosmetics, shampoo, soap, and hangers are cheap (13 hangers/$1 at Walmart) and universities run an orientation shopping trip. Don't waste weight on bulk toiletries.
items.weightNote: starter kit only; bulk toiletries waste weight

items.detail: Buy stationery locally and buy/borrow textbooks in the US (or use the library / PDFs). Don't pack heavy books — use an e-reader. Textbooks and heavy reading material are explicitly on the skip list.
items.weightNote: heavy books are a classic packing regret

items.detail: Carry modest emergency cash — universities suggest ~$200-300 per person for the first days (taxi, SIM, food) plus a forex/travel card. RBI permits up to $3,000 cash. Don't carry large sums.
